attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of painful thickening of the skin on the soles of her feet, particularly affecting the heels and balls of the feet. It is not pruiritic.The symptoms began shortly after her last menstrual period 6 months ago. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She works as a tour guide, spending long hours walking.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are symmetrical, yellow, hyperkeratotic plaques on the weight-bearing areas of both feet. The lesions are tender to palpation. There is similar thickening of the skin bilaterally on her hands.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features typical of keratoderma climactericum (haxthausen's disease), a condition characterised by painful hyperkeratosis of pressure points on the feet that typically develops in menopausal women. The symmetric distribution, onset coinciding with menopause, and occupational history of prolonged standing are classic features. It can also be associated with obesity, arthritis of the joints and hypertension. Lesions begin typically on pressure-loaded areas of the feet as round hyperkeratotic areas, and then become erythematous and painful with fissures. It can also involve the palms. The hyperkeratosis does not extend elsewhere than the palms and soles.

Topical salicylic acid is the most appropriate first-line treatment. As a keratolytic agent, it helps to break down the hyperkeratotic plaques and provides symptomatic relief. It should be combined with regular use of emollients to maintain skin hydration.

Oral antifungal therapy would not be appropriate as the primary treatment as this is not a fungal condition. While fungal infections can complicate keratoderma, there are no signs of fungal infection in this case.

Topical high-dose corticosteroid is not appropriate as first-line therapy as it may thin the skin and worsen symptoms in the long term. Additionally, there is no significant inflammatory component that would benefit from corticosteroid treatment.

Oral retinoids such as acitretin might be considered in severe cases that are resistant to topical therapy, but they are not first-line treatment due to their side effect profile and the need for monitoring.

Surgical debridement may provide temporary relief but does not address the underlying condition and should be reserved for cases where conservative measures have failed. It may be useful as an adjunctive treatment to improve the effectiveness of topical therapies.

Topical oestrogen has also shown to have benefit to reduce the keratosis. Maintenance involves loosing weight, using moisturiser and avoiding wearing sandals.
learningPoints: Keratoderma climactericum typically presents in postmenopausal women with painful hyperkeratosis of the pressure points of the feet.
Topical keratolytics and emollients are first-line treatment for keratoderma climactericum.
references: Lim D, Jibrael H. Keratoderma climactericum. The Australasian College of Dermatologists. 2016.  Available at: https://www.dermcoll.edu.au/atoz/keratoderma-climactericum/

Norman T, Obed O, Chen A, Worswick S. Palmoplantar keratoderma climactericum successfully treated with topical oestrogen.  JEADV Clinical Practice. 2023: 3 (1).  Available at: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/jvc2.277
